findings.title: `get_send_file_max_age` receives a raw PathLike object when called from `send_static_file`
findings.detail: `send_static_file` at line 987 calls `self.get_send_file_max_age(filename)` before passing `filename` to `send_from_directory`. At this point, `filename` may still be a PathLike object (e.g. `FakePath('index.html')`), because the fspath conversion only happens inside `send_from_directory`. The default `get_send_file_max_age` implementation just returns a constant, but subclasses that do string operations on the filename (e.g. checking the file extension with `name.lower().endswith('.js')`) will break. The `fspath()` conversion should happen at the entry point of `send_static_file`, before the call to `get_send_file_max_age`. The test at line 707 (`app.send_static_file(FakePath('index.html'))`) does not exercise a custom `get_send_file_max_age`, so this is untested.

findings.title: `hasattr(…, '__fspath__')` used instead of calling `fspath()` unconditionally, causing inconsistency with `send_from_directory`
findings.detail: In `send_file`, the PathLike conversion is guarded by `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')`, but in `send_from_directory` (lines 715-716) the conversion is done with an unconditional `fspath()` call (which handles the case where there is no `__fspath__` by returning the object unchanged). This inconsistency means the two code paths have different semantics. More critically, `os.fspath()` is the canonical way to convert PathLike objects; checking `__fspath__` manually and then calling `fspath()` anyway is redundant — just call `fspath()` directly. This is also a correctness issue: if someone passes a `bytes`-returning PathLike, the `hasattr` guard will match but `fspath()` will return `bytes` (on Python 3.6+), which will then fail the `isinstance(filename_or_fp, string_types)` check and fall into the file-object branch, silently treating the path as a file object.

findings.title: Fallback `fspath()` does not validate the return type like `os.fspath()` does
findings.detail: The real `os.fspath()` (Python 3.6+) raises `TypeError` if an object's `__fspath__` does not return a `str` or `bytes`. The fallback implementation in the `except ImportError` block simply returns whatever `path.__fspath__()` returns, with no type check. If a user provides a PathLike whose `__fspath__` returns a non-string (e.g. an int or another custom object), the fallback will silently pass the bad value along to `send_file`, which will then fail with a confusing error later rather than a clear `TypeError` at the `fspath` call-site. Additionally, if `path` is a plain `str` or `bytes` (i.e. already a path), `os.fspath()` returns it as-is, but the fallback also does this correctly via the `else` branch. This is still a behavioural difference from the stdlib implementation that should be documented or fixed.

findings.title: Missing `fspath` call for `send_from_directory` in docstring changelog
findings.detail: The docstring for `send_from_directory` (lines 688-713) does not include a `.. versionchanged:: 1.1` note for PathLike support, even though `send_file` has one. Users reading the `send_from_directory` docs will not know PathLike objects are now accepted.

findings.title: Redundant double-lookup: `hasattr(…, '__fspath__')` then `fspath()`
findings.detail: The check `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` followed immediately by `fspath(filename_or_fp)` is redundant. `fspath()` already handles objects without `__fspath__` (the fallback returns them unchanged). A single unconditional call to `fspath()` is cleaner and keeps the logic consistent with the `send_from_directory` implementation.

findings.title: Test for `send_file` with PathLike does not cover the `as_attachment` path or `bytes`-returning PathLike
findings.detail: `test_send_file_pathlike` only tests the happy path (reading a file, checking MIME type and data). There is no test covering: (1) a PathLike whose `__fspath__` returns `bytes` (important because `os.fspath` on Python 3.6 accepts both `str` and `bytes` returns), (2) a PathLike passed alongside `as_attachment` to confirm `attachment_filename` is set correctly, (3) a PathLike passed to `send_file` as an absolute path.

findings.title: Changelog entry contains a typo: 'as describe' should be 'as described'
findings.detail: Line 18 reads '`send_file` supports `PathLike` objects as describe in PEP 0519'. The correct phrasing is 'as described in'.
